parentQuestion: A patient's neurons are unable to fire action potentials after exposure to a tox...

scaffolds.q: Does the sodium-potassium pump require ATP to function?
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The Na⁺/K⁺ pump is an active transporter — it moves ions AGAINST their concentration gradients, which requires energy input (ATP). It's one of the most ATP-hungry processes in your body. Without ATP, it stops, and neurons lose their ability to fire. Expensive but essential.

scaffolds.q: In a resting neuron, is the concentration of Na⁺ higher inside or outside the cell?
scaffolds.explain: OUTSIDE! The Na⁺/K⁺ pump maintains HIGH sodium OUTSIDE the cell (~145 mM extracellular vs. ~12 mM intracellular). This gradient is what allows Na⁺ to rush IN during action potentials, causing depolarization. Beautiful ionic architecture, isn't it?
